subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 2: Is Matter Around Us Pure

key_points: Matter can be pure (elements or compounds) or mixtures
Mixtures can be homogeneous (solutions) or heterogeneous (suspensions, colloids)
Solutions have solute completely dissolved in solvent, showing no Tyndall effect
Suspensions have large particles that settle on standing
Colloids have intermediate-sized particles; they show Tyndall effect but do not settle
Alloys are homogeneous mixtures of metals or metals with non-metals and cannot be separated physically
Saturated solutions contain maximum solute at a given temperature
Separation techniques include filtration, evaporation, distillation, centrifugation, chromatography, and crystallization
examples: Sugar in water is a solution (homogeneous mixture)
Oil in water is a suspension (heterogeneous mixture)
Milk is a colloid showing Tyndall effect
Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc
Salt crystallization from seawater demonstrates separation of a pure substance from solution
formulas: Solubility = Mass of solute / Volume of solvent × 100
Saturated solution: solute present = maximum solubility at given temperature
exam_tips: Classify mixtures as homogeneous or heterogeneous
Identify solute, solvent, and type of mixture
Distinguish between solution, suspension, and colloid using Tyndall effect
Use solubility and saturation concepts in numerical problems
